title: CDIAC Meeting Summary - April 11, 2024
summary: The Community Depository Institutions Advisory Council met on April 11, 2024 and reported that the economy remains surprisingly resilient despite persistent inflationary pressures driven by strong consumer demand. Council members noted a bifurcation between upper- and lower-income groups, with lower-income households experiencing greater difficulty keeping up with rising prices. Inflation has moderated from its peak but remains stubbornly high, with services inflation persisting due to labor costs while goods inflation is slowing.
key_points: Economy remains resilient but faces inflationary pressures from strong consumer demand
Lower-income households are disproportionately affected by inflation, with increased requests for food assistance
Services inflation has persisted due to labor costs while goods inflation is moderating
Labor market conditions vary by region and industry; blue-collar labor remains challenging to find
Labor costs have moderated from 6-7% pay increases last year to 4-5% this year
Housing affordability remains restrictive across Districts despite some moderation in price increases
Technology costs have accelerated from 2-3% annual increases to as much as 10% in 2024
Council members expressed doubt about near-term feasibility of the 2% inflation target
Regional variations in housing conditions exist, with some areas loosening while others remain tight
Immigration is helping to fill labor vacuums in some regions
why_it_matters: This CDIAC summary provides a qualitative, ground-level assessment of economic conditions from the perspective of community depository institutions. The views on inflation persistence, labor market tightness, and housing affordability offer insights into conditions that may influence Federal Reserve policy deliberations. The skepticism about achieving the 2% inflation target in the near term is particularly notable.
macro_context: The document provides regional qualitative input on economic conditions including inflation, labor markets, and housing as of April 2024. Services inflation driven by labor costs remains persistent while goods inflation moderates. Labor markets are becoming more balanced between employers and employees.
important_numbers: 2% inflation target (expressed doubt about near-term feasibility)

human_review.notes: Source is a qualitative advisory council summary, not an official policy document. Historical record from April 2024 (730+ days old per TODAY of 2026-04-12). Confidence lowered due to age and qualitative nature.

notes: Historical record (published 2024-04-11, approximately 731 days before TODAY 2026-04-12). CDIAC meeting summary providing qualitative regional economic intelligence rather than official policy output. Contains anecdotal observations from community depository institutions regarding inflation, labor markets, and housing. Source body was truncated. Document does not contain formal policy signals or rate guidance from the Federal Reserve.
